Per-sample assessments (objective visual description)

Sample A (Image 1)
- Color and transparency: Central pale-to-medium yellow core with a lighter whitish-yellow rim; overall semi-transparent with an opaque-looking core and more translucent periphery.
- Clots/fibrin/coagulation structures: Yes. The material forms a cohesive, gelatinous mass that holds shape. A thin membranous “skirt” at the edge with faint striations suggests a fibrinous network; no discrete free-floating strands seen.
- Coagulation stage (visual inference): full_coagulation (gel-like clot).
- Density and homogeneity: Non-uniform; denser central dome, thinner peripheral film; visible gradient of density.
- Unique features: Dome-shaped gel with a thin, translucent rim and subtle surface striations; smooth convex surface with specular highlights.

Sample B (Image 2)
- Color and transparency: Deep straw/golden yellow; semi-transparent to mildly turbid (light transmission reduced, background not sharply visible).
- Clots/fibrin/coagulation structures: No discrete clots or strands visible; surface meniscus smooth.
- Coagulation stage (visual inference): none (no visible coagulation structures).
- Density and homogeneity: Appears homogeneous throughout the column; no visible layering or aggregates.
- Unique features: Uniform column with a thin, pale surface meniscus; no obvious particulate matter.

Sample C (Image 3)
Note: Image 3 shows three adjacent tubes. For consistency with “Image 3 → Sample C,” I assess the rightmost tube (third in the frame) as Sample C.
- Color and transparency: Straw yellow, slightly paler than Sample B; clearer (better light transmission) than the adjacent tubes.
- Clots/fibrin/coagulation structures: None visible; no discrete strands or pellets; smooth surface.
- Coagulation stage (visual inference): none.
- Density and homogeneity: Homogeneous, no visible layering or aggregates; appears slightly less turbid than the other tubes in the same frame.
- Unique features: Subtle slanted surface film at the air–liquid interface; comparatively higher clarity.

Comparison
- Most advanced coagulation: Sample A (only sample forming a cohesive gel/solid).
- Least coagulated: Sample C (clearest, no visible structures) — marginally less turbid than Sample B.
- Visible differences:
  - A is a semi-solid gel with clear density gradients and a membranous rim.

Per-sample assessments

Sample A (Image 1)
- Color and transparency: Bright/pale yellow; supernatant appears clear; the lower mass is more turbid → overall semi-transparent.
- Clots/fibrin: Yes. A cohesive, wedge-shaped gelatinous mass adheres to the bottom/side; no obvious wispy fibrin strands; a few tiny bubbles/particles present.

- Density/homogeneity: Two-phase. Low-density clear liquid layer over a denser, more opaque gel; heterogeneous.

Sample B (Image 2)
- Color and transparency: Pale yellow; turbid gel → semi-transparent to locally opaque in the center.
- Clots/fibrin: Yes. A rounded, dome-like clot firmly attached to the wall; thin peripheral film; a few dark specks trapped inside.

- Unique features: Spherical/ovoid, well-circumscribed clot with a denser center.

Sample C (Image 3)

- Clots/fibrin: Yes. Broad, continuous membrane-like clot covering a large area; faint internal lamellar lines; no free liquid visible.

- Density/homogeneity: Thin, relatively uniform layer; less compact than Sample B’s central core.

Comparison
- Most advanced coagulation: Sample B (densest, most compact clot).
- Least advanced coagulation: Sample A (clear two-phase system with free liquid).
- Visible differences: Sample A shows prominent phase separation with a bottom gel; Sample B shows a compact, dome-shaped clot; Sample C shows a large, thin, uniform sheet-like clot. Differences in clot thickness, compactness, and amount of free liquid are evident.
- Overall visual difference between samples: pronounced.
